title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Location
PDS 70 is located at a distance of 113.43 ± 0.52 pc and is a member of the Scorpius–Centaurus stellar association, specifically of the Upper Centaurus–Lupus (UCL) subgroup. One of the pieces of evidence that this is a young system is that, as the authors note, “Strong lithium absorption and the presence of a protoplanetary disk provide evidence of a young age” (<10 Myr) for PDS 70 (Gregorio-Hetem and Hetem 2002; Metchev and Hillenbrand 2004).

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
General Description of the System
PDS 70 is a young system composed of a pre-main-sequence T Tauri star of spectral type K7, surrounded by a transition protoplanetary disk. It is an ideal laboratory for studying planet formation because it brings together, within a single system, an inner disk, a large gap, an outer ring, and protoplanets.
The inner disk is a compact component located very close to the star, inside the large gap. Earlier observations described it as “a small optically thick inner disk emitting in the NIR” (Hashimoto et al. 2012; Dong et al. 2012). Later, this component was shown to be detected in the continuum within ~9 au and in gas out to ~15 au (Keppler et al. 2019), indicating that the innermost region still retains material close to the star.
The large gap corresponds to the radial cavity that separates the inner disk from the outer disk. Its structure was confirmed when the authors stated that “we confirm the detection of a large gap of ∼54 au in size within the disk in our scattered light images” (Keppler et al. 2018). The gap also shows evidence of radial dust segregation, since “The gap is found to be larger at sub-mm wavelengths” (Keppler et al. 2019).
The outer disk, in turn, is observed as a bright ring whose main emission peak is located at “The best-fit model has a peak radial position of 73.7 ± 0.1 au” (Keppler et al. 2019).
Finally, the system is especially remarkable because of the direct detection of protoplanets within the gap. Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2018) reported “a point source within the gap of the disk at about 195 mas (∼22 au) projected separation”, which was identified as PDS 70 b. Later, Haffert et al. (Haffert et al. 2019) detected Hα emission at two locations in the system and concluded: “We therefore identify this second source of Hα emission as the location of a second, forming planet, PDS 70 c”. The evidence for active accretion in both objects is based on the fact that “The Hα emission spectra of both proto-planets indicate ongoing accretion onto the proto-planets” (Haffert et al. 2019).
Overall, PDS 70 stands out as a valuable laboratory for planet formation because it combines a large cavity, an inner disk, a well-resolved outer ring, and two actively accreting protoplanets within the gap.

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Disk Morphology
PDS 70 exhibits the characteristic morphology of a transition disk, but with an especially rich internal structure. The system includes a compact inner disk, a large cavity, and a well-resolved outer ring. In polarized light, the outer disk appears as an elliptical ring with a non-uniform brightness distribution. ALMA observations, however, reveal a more complex arrangement of dust and gas, including a main ring at ~74 au, a possible additional inner component, and a tentative bridge- or spur-like structure that may connect the outer and inner regions of the disk. Located within the large gap are the two protoplanets PDS 70 b and PDS 70 c, which makes this system a particularly important case for studying planet–disk interaction and planet formation in progress.

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Dust in the disk
The dust in PDS 70 is not evenly distributed, but instead organized into distinct disk substructures. Beyond the inner disk lies the gap, a region where the dust emission is strongly depleted. Farther out, ALMA millimeter continuum observations, which trace larger dust grains, show that this material is primarily concentrated in an outer ring peaking at 73.7 ± 0.1 au, with a possible additional component near 60 au. Taken together, these observations indicate that small grains remain closer to the cavity edge, whereas larger grains accumulate farther out, consistent with a dust-trapping scenario (Keppler et al. 2018, 2019).

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Gas in the disk
The gas component in PDS 70 has been studied primarily through the \(^{12}\)CO \(J=3-2\) line. Unlike the dust continuum, this emission traces an elevated layer of the disk rather than the midplane, so its observed morphology appears more extended and geometrically more complex. ALMA observations show that the gaseous cavity is not completely empty: there is an inner depression in emission near 23 au, an inner gaseous disk extending out to about 15 au, and a possible structure linking the inner disk to the outer ring in the northwestern region. In addition, the gas kinematics show departures from Keplerian rotation inside ~0.8″ (91 au), suggesting the presence of pressure gradients within the disk. Taken together, these results indicate that the gas is depleted near the orbit of PDS 70 b, but not absent, and may still be flowing between the outer disk and the inner regions (Keppler et al. 2019).

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
PDS 70 provides some of the clearest evidence of ongoing planet formation in a protoplanetary disk. First, Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2018) detected PDS 70 b as a point source inside the large disk gap, at a separation of about 195 mas (~22 au), and follow-up observations confirmed that it is physically associated with the system. Its photometry is also consistent with a planetary-mass companion. Second, Haffert et al. (Haffert et al. 2019) detected Hα emission from both PDS 70 b and PDS 70 c. Because Hα traces accreting gas, this shows that both protoplanets are still actively gaining material and continue to grow. Third, Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2019) found that the disk itself is being perturbed: the CO emission shows a depression near the orbit of PDS 70 b, together with a deviation from Keplerian rotation that reveals changes in the gas pressure structure. Taken together, these results show that PDS 70 is not only a disk with substructures, but a system where planet formation is directly observed in action.

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Physical interpretation
PDS 70 is interpreted as a young planetary system that is still forming, in which the protoplanets are already actively reshaping the disk structure. The large cavity, the segregation between small and large dust grains, and the presence of an inner disk led Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2018) to identify the system early on as a strong candidate for harboring planets capable of carving the gap. Later, ALMA observations showed that the gas displays a depression near the orbit of PDS 70 b, together with a departure from Keplerian rotation consistent with a pressure gradient that can explain why the main dust ring lies much farther out; however, Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2019) also concluded that a single planet may not be enough to reproduce the full observed morphology, suggesting a more complex system architecture, possibly including an additional low-mass companion. The later Hα detection of PDS 70 c further strengthens this picture: both protoplanets are accreting, and their orbits are consistent with a near 2:1 resonance configuration, making PDS 70 a particularly valuable system for studying planet–disk interaction and migration in young planetary systems (Haffert et al. 2019).

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
IM Lup is associated with the Lupus 2 cloud and lies at a distance of 161 ± 10 pc (Cleeves et al. 2016). Its location in a nearby star-forming region and its young age make it a useful system for studying how gas and dust evolve differently during the early stages of protoplanetary disk evolution.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Overview of the system
IM Lup is a young, massive, and extended protoplanetary disk whose gas and dust components show markedly different spatial distributions (Cleeves et al. 2016). ALMA observations of the continuum, \(^{12}\)CO, \(^{13}\)CO, and C\(^{18}\)O were used together with physical and chemical modeling to constrain the structure of the disk.
A key result of the paper is that the millimeter dust disk is truncated at about 313 au, while the gas disk extends to at least 570 au and perhaps as far as 970 au (Cleeves et al. 2016). This contrast indicates that large grains are more radially compact than the gas, consistent with dust growth, settling, and radial drift.
The study also found tentative evidence for weak ring-like substructure in the millimeter continuum and showed that the disk is both massive and flared, properties that may be related to its young age (Cleeves et al. 2016).

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
ALMA observations show that IM Lup has a two-component millimeter dust structure: a bright inner disk and a broad outer dust halo truncated at 313 au (Cleeves et al. 2016). In contrast, the gas traced by CO isotopologues is much more extended and appears smoother in radial distribution.
The continuum data also show shallow deviations from a smooth radial profile, suggesting tentative ring-like substructure. These features are weak and were not resolved as sharply as the rings later seen in higher-resolution disk surveys, but they are still morphologically important because they hint that substructure may already be present in IM Lup (Cleeves et al. 2016).
The paper describes these features as small breaks in the deprojected continuum brightness profile near 150 au and 250 au.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
Dust in IM Lup was observed with ALMA continuum data at 1.3 mm and 875 μm (Cleeves et al. 2016). These observations trace the distribution of large, millimeter-sized grains, which are more concentrated than the gas and appear sharply truncated at 313 au.
The continuum emission is dominated by a bright central component plus a broad outer dust distribution. The paper models the large-grain dust disk as being more radially compact and more vertically settled than the gas. This is consistent with the idea that large grains have already evolved significantly through growth, settling toward the midplane, and radial drift (Cleeves et al. 2016).
The inner dust disk is also inferred to be optically thick, which helps explain the reduced line emission seen near the center in some CO isotopologue maps.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
The gas component of IM Lup was observed in \(^{12}\)CO, \(^{13}\)CO, and C\(^{18}\)O transitions (Cleeves et al. 2016). These lines show that the gas disk is much more extended than the millimeter dust disk.
The paper finds that the gas disk extends to at least 570 au and possibly to ~970 au, making IM Lup one of the clearest examples of a large dust–gas size difference in a protoplanetary disk (Cleeves et al. 2016).
The gas structure appears relatively smooth compared with the dust continuum. In addition, the central low-velocity channels of \(^{12}\)CO J = 2–1 show a diffuse outer component or halo, which may be associated with extended gas beyond the main disk.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
At this stage, Cleeves et al. (2016) do not provide direct evidence for embedded protoplanets in IM Lup. However, the paper does identify processes that are strongly connected to early planet formation.
The strong difference between the compact millimeter dust disk and the much larger gas disk is consistent with grain growth, settling, and radial drift, which are key steps in the evolution from dust grains to planetesimals (Cleeves et al. 2016). The tentative ring-like breaks at ~150 au and ~250 au are also morphologically interesting, although the authors emphasize that higher-resolution observations are needed to confirm them.
For now, this paper provides indirect evidence of dust evolution relevant to planet formation, rather than direct evidence of planets opening gaps.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation of IM Lup
Cleeves et al. (2016) interpret IM Lup as a young and massive disk in which gas and dust are already evolving differently (Cleeves et al. 2016). The large grains are more compact radially and more settled vertically than the gas, while the gas remains extended far into the outer disk.
The study concludes that the dust structure can be understood as a bright inner disk plus a broad outer halo truncated at 313 au, whereas the gas extends to much larger radii. This contrast supports a picture in which the solid component is undergoing substantial evolution, even though the system is still very young.
The paper also suggests that the disk may retain some surrounding diffuse material, since the low-velocity \(^{12}\)CO emission includes a halo-like component that could be related to a remnant envelope, captured material, or other extended gas structures. Even so, the main morphological result remains the same: IM Lup shows a clear decoupling between the spatial distributions of gas and millimeter dust (Cleeves et al. 2016).

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
HD 163296 is a young Herbig Ae star located at a distance of approximately 101–122 parsecs (~330–400 light-years) from Earth (Isella et al. 2016; Rab et al. 2020).
The star belongs to the class of intermediate-mass pre–main sequence stars and is surrounded by one of the most extensively studied protoplanetary disks. Because the disk is large, bright, and relatively isolated, it provides an ideal laboratory for investigating the processes that shape planet-forming disks.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text: General description of the system

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
section: HD 163296 is a protoplanetary disk surrounding a Herbig Ae star whose dust morphology has made it a benchmark system for the study of radial substructures; indeed, it has been described as “the third system known to host multiple rings in dust emission at millimetre wavelength” (Diep et al., 2019). On global scales, the system combines a large, dynamically ordered gas disk—“The gas disc is in Keplerian motion and has a radius of about 550 au, and the millimetre continuum emission from solid particles is confined to within 250 au of the star”—with a more compact solid component, where earlier observations resolved “three concentric gap-ring pairs,” with gaps at ~54, ~100, and 160 au (Diep et al., 2019). Higher-resolution observations further show that this architecture is not limited to simple concentric rings: “The continuum observations resolve and allow us to characterize the previously discovered dust rings at radii of 68 and 100. They also reveal new small-scale structures, such as a dark gap at 10 au, a bright ring at 15 au, a dust crescent at a radius of 55 au, and several fainter azimuthal asymmetries” (Isella et al., 2018). In this sense, the dust distribution is not uniform but exhibits both radial and azimuthal variations, since “The dust continuum emission is characterized by several asymmetric structures,” and these features “corroborate the hypothesis that the complex structure of the HD 163296 disk is the result of the gravitational interaction with yet-unseen planets” (Isella et al., 2018).

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
section: The morphology of HD 163296 can be described as that of a dust disk with a hierarchical architecture of rings and gaps: in the 1.3 mm continuum, Diep et al. (2019) note the presence of “three concentric gap-ring pairs,” and that at higher resolution the disk shows “much deeper gaps” between the rings, more clearly defining the radial redistribution of dust. To this global structure, Isella et al. (2018) add “three new morphological features,” including an inner dark gap at ~10 au, a bright ring at ~15 au, and a central azimuthal asymmetry, while in the outer disk “the outermost features B67, D86, B100, D145, and B155, are well described by concentric circles.” Beyond this concentric organization, the disk is not perfectly uniform: “the image of the residuals shows fainter asymmetries along the B67 and B100 dust rings” (Isella et al., 2018), and Diep et al. (2019) further conclude that the outer gap and outer ring display a “significant modulation of their morphology and intensity.” Altogether, HD 163296 exhibits a morphology in which gaps, rings, and local asymmetries coexist within a globally thin and well-organized disk structure.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text: Dust in the disk

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
section: In the case of HD 163296, dust dynamics—that is, the radial migration of solids toward local gas-pressure maxima—is interpreted as a key mechanism in the formation of the rings. These bright rings—“the rings B67 and B100 of the HD 163296 disk, have a radial width that is narrower than the estimated pressure scale height of the gaseous disk” (Isella et al. 2018)—suggest that the dust is concentrated and confined within relatively narrow regions. In addition to ring and gap structures, the disk also shows asymmetric dust concentrations: “new small-scale structures, such as a dark gap at 10 au, a bright ring at 15 au, a dust crescent at a radius of 55 au, and several fainter azimuthal asymmetries” (Isella et al. 2018). Among these asymmetries, the most prominent correspond to crescents, since “The dust continuum emission is characterized by several asymmetric structures. The most prominent consist in crescents centered at about 4 and 55 au” (Isella et al. 2018). For this reason, the dusty environment of the disk should be understood as dynamic rather than perfectly axisymmetric. This interpretation is further reinforced by the kinematic evidence presented by Pinte et al. (2020), who note that “upon deprojection, we find that all of the candidate planets lie within a gap and/or at the end of a spiral detected in dust continuum emission.” In other words, the dust substructures not only describe where solid material is concentrated or redistributed, but also spatially coincide with possible embedded perturbers, supporting the idea that part of the observed dust architecture is being sculpted by forming protoplanets.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text: Gas in the disk

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
section: According to the gas structure of HD 163296, the gaseous disk does not end where the millimeter dust continuum is observed, but instead extends much farther outward: “The CO emission extends far beyond the outer edge of millimeter-wave continuum emission up to an angular distance of 5 6, or about 560 au, from the central star” (Isella et al. 2018). To understand how its geometry is observed, it is important to note that the CO-traced gas does not appear as a single layer, but as two warm emitting surfaces on the front and rear sides of the disk: “The CO emission observed at intermediate velocities … shows a characteristic ‘dragonfly’ structure tracing both the front and rear warm CO-emitting layers of the disk” (Isella et al. 2018). The rear-side emission appears fainter because “The emission coming from the rear of the disk is fainter than that from the front due to a combination of lower temperature of the emitting gas and absorption from the intervening material” (Isella et al. 2018). The separation between these two surfaces therefore makes it possible to infer the vertical structure of the gas, since “the separation between the front and rear CO emission measures the vertical geometry of the CO-emitting surfaces” (Isella et al. 2018). In addition, the CO temperature decreases with distance from the star, reaching values “as high as 120 K in the innermost disk regions and as low as 20 K at the disk outer edge” (Isella et al. 2018). Finally, the global motion of the gas follows an ordered rotation dominated by stellar gravity: “The overall kinematics of the CO gas is well described by Keplerian rotation around a star with a mass of about 2.0 M⊙” (Isella et al. 2018). Unlike the dust, the gas does not trace prominent rings and gaps, but instead appears in a smoother way, since “The maps of the 12CO, 13CO, and C18O J = 2 − 1 emission do not show such structures but reveal a change in the slope of the radial intensity profile across the positions of the dark rings in the continuum image” (Isella et al. 2016). This shows that what matters is not only how much gas is present, but also how it is redistributed relative to the dust, because “we deduce that the gas-to-dust ratio varies across the disk and, in particular, it increases by at least a factor 5 within the inner dust gap compared to adjacent regions of the disk” (Isella et al. 2016), suggesting that in this inner region the dust is more depleted than the gas.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text: Evidence of planet formation

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
section: The architecture of HD 163296, particularly its gaps and rings, is linked to real perturbations in the disk gas rather than to changes in dust opacity alone, as shown by the fact that “The recent detection of deviations from Keplerian rotation at the location of the gaps and rings by Teague et al. (2018) confirms that these structures correspond to variations in the gas pressure (i.e., variations in the gas density and/or temperature), as opposed to radial changes in the dust opacity” (Isella et al. 2018). In this context, comparisons between the observations and planet–disk interaction models suggest that several of these gaps may have been carved by planets, since “The comparison between the observations and planet–disk interaction models indicates that the density gaps might have been carved by planets with masses between 0.5 and 1 MJ orbiting at 48, 86, and 131 au from the central star” (Isella et al. 2018). The paper also shows that this evidence does not rely on a single scenario, because under very low-viscosity conditions “the observed multiple ring structure may also result from the gravitational interaction with a single planet that is less massive than Saturn at 100 au orbital radius” (Isella et al. 2018). In addition, substructures such as the crescent observed inside D45 reinforce this interpretation, since “features similar to the crescent observed inside the D45 gap (inset b of Figure 1) are naturally produced by the planet–disk interaction whenever the disk viscosity is low (α < 10−3) and the planet mass is above about 0.1 MJ” (Isella et al. 2018). Finally, the continuum asymmetries are not interpreted merely as morphological details, but as further support for this hypothesis, because “these asymmetries trace local variations in the dust densities and support the hypothesis that the HD 163296 disk is shaped by the gravitational interaction with yet-unseen planets” (Isella et al. 2018). This interpretation is extended by recent dynamical studies, in which HD 163296 “has been proposed to host at least four giant planets on wide orbits as well as a massive planetesimal disc” (Polychroni et al. 2025), so that the evidence for planet formation is not limited to the observed morphology, but also includes its consequences for the solid material in the system. Within that framework, “in our simulations the formation of giant planets in the HD 163296 system creates a large population of dynamically excited planetesimals, the majority of which originate from beyond the CO snowline” (Polychroni et al. 2025), and “HD 163296’s giant planets between 50 and 260 au are capable of delivering large amounts of planetesimals inwards of 40 au” (Polychroni et al. 2025), suggesting that the presence of young giant planets may not only sculpt rings and gaps, but also redistribute material toward the inner regions of the system.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text: Physical interpretation of HD 163296

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
section: HD 163296 can be physically interpreted as a disk in which dust is not distributed uniformly, but instead tends to accumulate in specific regions of the gas. In particular, “Dullemond et al. (2018) finds that many of the continuum rings revealed by DSHARP observations, including the rings B67 and B100 of the HD 163296 disk, have a radial width that is narrower than the estimated pressure scale height of the gaseous disk, and conclude that this is strong evidence that these rings have formed by radial migration of dust particles toward local maxima of the gas pressure” (Isella et al. 2018). This suggests that the rings are not simply patterns seen in the image, but regions where dust has migrated and accumulated because the local gas pressure favors it. At the same time, this architecture develops within a thin disk, and the outer region is not perfectly uniform, since “The disc is shown to be thin … Significant modulation of the intensity of the outer ring emission is revealed and discussed” (Diep et al. 2019). This complexity cannot be explained by smooth dust trapping alone, because “the observed asymmetries in the dust continuum emission corroborate the hypothesis that the complex structure of the HD 163296 disk is the result of the gravitational interaction with yet-unseen planets” (Isella et al. 2018). Meanwhile, the gas preserves an overall ordered motion, given that “The three line emissions display remarkably similar kinematics dominated by Keplerian rotation. An upper limit of 9% of the rotation velocity has been placed on a possible in-fall velocity” (Diep et al. 2019). Taken together, these results suggest a system in which the rings arise from dust concentration at gas-pressure maxima, while the more complex structures, such as asymmetries and brightness modulations, point to active interaction between the disk and possible forming planets.

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
AS 209 is located in the Ophiuchus molecular cloud, a nearby star-forming region that hosts many young stellar systems. In the literature, the source is described as a T Tauri star in the young Ophiuchus star-forming region at a distance of 126 pc from the Sun (Fedele et al. 2018).
Its relative proximity makes AS 209 an excellent target for high-resolution millimeter observations, allowing both the dust and gas substructures of the disk to be studied in detail.
##General description of the system
AS 209 is a young protoplanetary system surrounding a T Tauri star, with a stellar mass of 0.9 M☉, spectral type K5, and luminosity of 1.5 L☉ (Tazzari et al. 2016), located in the Ophiuchus star-forming region at a distance of 126 pc. At 1.3 mm, Fedele et al. (2018) show that its disk displays a bright central core, two prominent rings at ~75 and ~130 au, and two gaps at ~62 and ~103 au, with the inner gap being narrower and shallower than the outer one. Building on this observational picture, Zhang et al. (2018) highlight the “excellent agreement between observations and simulations for AS 209” and describe it as “a system with many gaps,” reinforcing the idea that it is a particularly valuable object for studying disk substructures and their possible connection to planet formation. In addition, the multiwavelength analysis of Tazzari et al. (2016) reveals evidence for radial dust evolution: the largest grains are concentrated in the inner regions, whereas smaller grains dominate the outer disk, suggesting that the growth and redistribution of solids do not occur uniformly throughout the disk.

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
AS 209 exhibits a radial architecture with multiple gaps, as noted by Zhang et al. (2018): “Huang et al. (2018a) and Guzmán et al. (2018) identified many gaps in this system including dark annuli at 9, 24, 35, 61, 90, 105, and 137 au.” Not all of these substructures have the same depth, depending on their location within the system. For example, the authors indicate that “We find that the gap at r = 61 au is shallower than the main gap, and it is at 0.5–0.7 rp. Thus, we treat it as a secondary gap induced by the planet.” In other words, the gap at 61 au would be less deep than the main gap around 100 au. Within that same interpretive framework, Zhang et al. (2018) add: “Our simulation with one planet at 99 au not only matches the primary gap around 100 au, but also matches the position and amplitude of secondary (61 au), tertiary (35 au), and even the fourth (24 au) inner gaps.” This morphology is also clearly reflected in the dust continuum observations presented by Fedele et al. (2018): “The dust continuum emission is characterized by a main central core and two prominent rings at r = 75 au and r = 130 au intervaled by two gaps at r = 62 au and r = 103 au.” The rings of AS 209 also show: “The continuum emission is characterized by a bright central emission and two weaker dust rings peaking at ∼75 and 130 au” (Fedele et al. 2018); thus, the continuum emission maxima are located in those two rings. The annular substructures in the system are not continuous, but are instead separated by narrow gaps with lower emission. In general, the architecture of AS 209 is described as follows: “We find that the 1.3 mm continuum brightness distribution of AS 209 can be explained very well by a profile with two deep gaps … at 62 and 103 au, respectively, and an excess ring … at ∼130 au” (Fedele et al. 2018).

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
The observation and study of dust dynamics in protoplanetary disks are grounded in the fact that “High angular resolution observations at several (sub-)mm wavelengths constitute the ideal tool with which to directly probe the bulk of dust grains and to investigate the radial distribution of their properties” (Tazzari et al., 2016). In this context, it is well established that dust is not distributed uniformly, since “Theoretical models of grain growth predict that dust properties change as a function of disk radius, mass, age, and other physical conditions” (Tazzari et al., 2016). In the case of AS 209, this radial variation is closely linked to grain size: “In these disks we find evidence of a decrease in the maximum dust grain size, amax, with radius. We derive large amax values up to 1 cm in the inner disk 15 AU ≤ R ≤ 30 AU and smaller grains with amax ∼ 1 mm in the outer disk (R ≳ 80 AU)” (Tazzari et al., 2016). Fedele et al. (2018) further show that this distribution changes not only with radius, but also from one disk substructure to another, since “the inner gap is partially filled with millimeter grains while the outer gap is largely devoid of dust,” indicating that dust depletion is not equally severe in all gaps. The reason for this behavior is that “radial drift effectively depletes the large grain population in disks within 1 Myr … unless it is halted by the occurrence of dust traps” (Tazzari et al., 2016); indeed, Fedele et al. explain that “the millimeter dust grains are radially confined regions in which inward radial migration is slowed down or stopped” and that “dust traps provide the ideal environment … allowing dust to grow in situ.” In AS 209, these dust traps help explain both the retention of large grains in the disk and their accumulation in rings, within a scenario in which “the planet is responsible for the gap opening and for the pile-up of dust at the outer edge of the planet orbit” (Fedele et al., 2018).

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
In AS 209, the gas is also not distributed uniformly, and its structure becomes clearer when different molecular tracers are examined together. The C\(^{18}\)O emission first reveals a well-defined radial organization, with an inner concentration of gas close to the star and an outer ring around 140 au, separated by a region of weaker emission: “The C18O emission shows a compact (≲60 au), centrally peaked emission and an outer ring peaking at 140 au” (Favre et al., 2019). In addition, its radial profile “harbors a drop in intensity between the two continuum gaps” (Favre et al., 2019), suggesting that the substructures observed in the dust are also reflected in the gas. This connection is further strengthened by Fedele et al. (2018), who note that “the ALMA C18O image presented in Huang et al. (2016) shows an extended emission peaking at nearly 130 au. This extended emission is co-spatial to the outer dust ring,” indicating that the outer dust ring also has a gaseous counterpart. Along the same line, the authors add that “This is a strong indication that the large scale C18O emission follows the actual disk surface density in the outer disk and it points to a gas density drop by a factor of a few inside the outer gap (‘G2’),” reinforcing the idea that the outer gap is not only a dust substructure, but also a region where the gas density decreases. In contrast, DCO\(^+\) does not follow exactly the same pattern: “Regarding the DCO+ emission, two rings are observed” (Favre et al., 2019), one between the two dust gaps and another beyond the outer gap, showing that different tracers reveal different physical and chemical conditions within the disk. To explain this overall morphology, Favre et al. conclude that “Σgas needs to be lowered” inside the gaps (Favre et al., 2019), meaning that temperature or opacity effects alone are not sufficient and that a real decrease in the gas surface density is required. Taken together, this evidence supports a dynamical interpretation of the disk, in which the gaseous structure of AS 209 has been modified by a “gas perturbation due to one or more forming planets” (Favre et al., 2019).

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
In AS 209, the evidence for planet formation does not rest on a single isolated substructure, but rather on the consistent way in which observations and models describe the disk as a dynamically perturbed system. In the millimeter continuum, it had already been proposed that “the observed rings and gaps of the 1.3 mm continuum emission are likely due to the formation of planets; this hypothesis is commensurate with hydrodynamical simulations” (Favre et al., 2019), meaning that the ringed architecture of the disk is consistent with the action of forming planets. This interpretation is further strengthened by the results of Zhang et al. (2018): “our simulation with one planet at 99 au not only matches the primary gap around 100 au, but also matches the position and amplitude of secondary (61 au), tertiary (35 au), and even the fourth (24 au) inner gaps” (Zhang et al., 2018). In other words, a single embedded planet could account not only for the main gap, but also for several inner substructures in the disk. For this reason, the authors conclude that “This makes AS 209 the most plausible case that there is indeed a planet within the 100 au gap” (Zhang et al., 2018). Moreover, the evidence does not come from the dust alone: Favre et al. (2019) add that “Our findings confirm that the previously observed dust gaps are very likely due to perturbation of the gas surface density that is induced by a planet of at least 0.2MJ in formation”, which reinforces the interpretation that the observed gaps trace a real planet–disk interaction process rather than a merely apparent redistribution of material.

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation of AS 209
AS 209 can be understood as a young disk in which dust evolution and planet–disk interaction are operating together. On the one hand, the dust does not evolve uniformly across the system: larger grains are concentrated toward the inner regions, while smaller grains dominate farther out in the disk, a pattern that is consistent with grain growth, radial drift, and the need for retention mechanisms to keep solids from being rapidly lost. On the other hand, the observed ring-and-gap architecture is best explained if the disk is being dynamically sculpted by an embedded planet in a very low-viscosity environment, since Zhang et al. show that “the q = 10−4 planet in a α = 10−5 and h/r = 0.05 disk can explain all five gaps at 24, 35, 62, 90, and 105 au,” implying that a single outer planet could account for much of the system’s radial structure. This interpretation becomes even stronger because the gas is also genuinely perturbed: Favre et al. conclude that “the previously observed dust gaps are very likely due to perturbation of the gas surface density that is induced by a planet of at least 0.2MJ in formation,” showing that the gaps are not merely brightness variations in the dust, but signatures of a real reorganization of the disk surface density. Taken together, AS 209 is best interpreted as a low-turbulence disk in which a forming planet is already reshaping both the dust and the gas, leaving an observable imprint of ongoing planet formation.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
AS 209 is located in the Ophiuchus star-forming region, at a distance of 126 pc from the Sun (Fedele et al. 2018). Ophiuchus is a particularly valuable environment for the study of protoplanetary disks, as it hosts a rich population of young stellar systems observed across different evolutionary stages. Its significant number of deeply embedded sources, many with ages below about 1 Myr, makes this region especially well suited for investigating how circumstellar disks emerge and evolve during the earliest phases of star and planet formation.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
General description of the system
HL Tau is a remarkably important young system because, although it is still described as a “Class I–II protostar” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), it already hosts a compact, massive disk together with a highly collimated outflow, reflecting an early yet highly active stage of evolution. ALMA observations revealed a clear sequence of “bright and dark rings” in the disk (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), showing that a complex architecture is already in place at this initial stage. Moreover, the dark regions are “not devoid of emission” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), and their properties are consistent with dust growth and evolution, suggesting that they may be linked to the earliest stages of planet formation. This interpretation becomes clearer when one considers that ALMA primarily traces millimeter-sized dust: in the presence of embedded protoplanets, that dust can respond differently from the gas, making the gaps appear sharper in the dust component (“different response of the dust compared to the gas”; “it is easier to carve gaps in dust than in gas” (Dipierro et al. 2015)). Along the same lines, radiative transfer modeling indicates that the main gaps are “depleted by at least a factor of 10” in dust and that the outermost ring contains fewer millimeter-sized grains than the inner regions, pointing to more efficient grain growth closer to the star and/or radial migration (Pinte et al. 2016). In addition, the millimeter dust forms a “geometrically thin (scale height ≈1 AU at 100 AU)” layer (Pinte et al. 2016), which is interpreted as a clear sign of strong settling toward the midplane, precisely where solid material can become concentrated and continue to evolve.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
The morphology of the HL Tau disk is defined by a remarkably pronounced annular architecture, resolved by ALMA in unprecedented detail at 2.9, 1.3, and 0.87 mm. These observations reveal a pattern of “bright and dark rings observed at all wavelengths” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), demonstrating that these are genuine disk substructures rather than imaging artifacts. In the combined 1.0 mm image, the authors identify “seven pairs of ‘bright’ and ‘dark’ rings” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), organized as B1–B7 for the bright rings and D1–D7 for the dark gaps, revealing a complex and highly ordered radial distribution of material. Dipierro et al. (2015) further describe HL Tau as showing “concentric, axisymmetric gaps,” emphasizing the regular and symmetric character of this morphology. In their interpretation, this appearance can be understood because ALMA primarily traces millimeter-sized dust, and that component does not respond to embedded planets in the same way as the gas; as a result, while the gas may develop spiral structure, the dust tends to highlight rings and gaps because of the “different response of gas and dust” (Dipierro et al. 2015). In addition, the planets in their model are not presented as direct detections, but rather as a dynamical test of plausibility: the main observed morphology can be reproduced with “three embedded planets at 13.2, 32.3 and 68.8 au” (Dipierro et al. 2015), supporting the idea that the most prominent gaps may be linked to planet–disk interaction.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
In HL Tau, dust is not distributed homogeneously, but instead exhibits a clearly differentiated radial and vertical structure. ALMA observations indicate a “pattern of bright and dark rings observed at all wavelengths,” and that the dark rings “are not devoid of emission” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), which indicates that the solid material does not disappear completely in those regions, but is instead redistributed within the disk. On the one hand, Pinte et al. show that the spectral index increases from the inner to the outer regions, indicating that the emission properties change with radius. In this context, the fact that the outermost ring is depleted in millimeter-sized dust is interpreted as evidence of “faster grain growth in the central regions and/or radial migration of the larger grains” (Pinte et al. 2016). Added to this is the fact that the main gaps show a drastic reduction in dust density relative to the adjacent rings, indicating that the solid material does not form a smooth disk, but is already strongly concentrated in some regions and depleted in others. Moreover, the millimeter dust is confined to a “geometrically thin (scale height ≈1 AU at 100 AU)” (Pinte et al. 2016) layer, which constitutes a clear sign of vertical settling toward the midplane. Taken together, this suggests that the millimeter continuum is dominated by large, settled grains, whereas reproducing the SED requires an additional population of submicron grains distributed in a much thicker structure, thus reinforcing the idea of strong dust stratification in the disk.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
In HL Tau, the disk gas is not observed as a simple, isolated component, but rather as part of a system that remains embedded in an active environment. ALMA observations show that its study is affected by “spatial and kinematic confusion due to the extended ridge and outflow emission near the ambient velocity of vlsr = 6–7 km s−1” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), meaning that the disk signal is partially blended with that of the surrounding material. Even so, HCO+ made it possible to “spatially resolve the morphology of the HL Tau molecular gas disk for the first time” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), revealing a “roughly Keplerian” velocity distribution over a detectable range of 2.0 to 12.0 km s−1. Along the same lines, Pinte et al. show that both HCO+ and ^12CO display “a spatially compact component” and a “velocity gradient consistent with disk rotation” (Pinte et al. 2016), confirming that the brightest gas is truly associated with the disk and rotates around the star. Moreover, not all tracers describe this dynamics in the same way: CN and HCN “are only detected in absorption” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), and part of the observed asymmetry is interpreted as self-absorption linked to the outflow. Taken together, this picture shows that the gas in HL Tau follows an approximately Keplerian rotation, although its structure and kinematics are not entirely simple; indeed, while ALMA Partnership derives a stellar mass of about ~1.3 M☉, Pinte et al. conclude that the gas kinematics are more consistent with a star of ~1.7 M☉, reinforcing the view that this is a young, dynamic molecular disk whose detailed interpretation still requires further modeling.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
Observationally, HL Tau provides evidence of planet formation: thanks to ALMA, we know that the disk material is already being dynamically sculpted. The disk gaps “are not devoid of emission” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), which means that the cavity is not empty, but instead corresponds to a region where the dust has been redistributed by a physical process consistent with planet–disk interaction. In addition, several rings show center offsets, and some substructures follow resonant proportions such as D1:D2:D3:D4 = 1:4:6:8; these dynamics—opening gaps, perturbing the orbits of disk material, and organizing structures within the disk—are evidence of a protoplanet altering the disk architecture. This interpretation is further supported by the simulations of Dipierro et al. (2015), in which “three embedded planets” were modeled at 13.2, 32.3, and 68.8 au, with masses of 0.2, 0.27, and 0.55 MJ, and it was concluded that this scenario can “reproduce most of the structures seen in the ALMA image”. In other words, the planets are not presented here as directly detected objects, but as a physically plausible explanation for why the disk displays such an ordered architecture: the protoplanets open gaps, push dust toward the edges of those gaps, and—because the dust responds more clearly than the gas—the final image observed by ALMA naturally takes on this morphology of bright and dark rings.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation
HL Tau can be physically interpreted as an extremely young system that remains in an active evolutionary stage, yet whose disk already exhibits a complex architecture that is unlikely to arise from a passive distribution of material alone. As ALMA observations describe it, HL Tau is “an excellent example of a system just emerging from its protostellar cocoon” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), and even at this stage it already shows bright rings, dark gaps, evidence of grain growth, and a radial organization suggesting that both dust and gas are being dynamically rearranged. In this context, Dipierro et al. provide a broader physical interpretation: the morphology of HL Tau can be understood as the outcome of interactions between dust, gas, and embedded planets, with the millimeter dust responding more visibly than the gas to gravitational perturbations and therefore producing the sharply defined rings and gaps seen by ALMA. Taken together, these studies converge on the same picture: HL Tau is not simply a young disk with substructures, but a system in which dust evolution, material redistribution, and the possible presence of protoplanets may already be actively sculpting the disk architecture.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
This Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog is an educational and scientific resource designed to explore the physical properties, morphologies, and evolutionary processes of protoplanetary disks.
The goal of this catalog is to organize observational and theoretical knowledge about young circumstellar disks using results from the scientific literature. Each disk entry summarizes its stellar properties, disk morphology, and observational signatures that may be related to planet formation.
The catalog focuses particularly on disk substructures, such as rings, gaps, spirals, and asymmetries, which have become central observational features in modern studies of planet-forming disks.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
section: Disk accretion and angular momentum transport
text:
Disk accretion and angular momentum transport
Gas in a disk orbits the central star approximately in Keplerian rotation. However, for material to accrete onto the star, it must lose angular momentum. This redistribution of angular momentum drives disk evolution.
Disk dynamics therefore play a central role in shaping disk structure. As Andrews (2020) notes:

“Disks are profoundly affected by their fluid dynamics.” (Andrews, 2020)

Theoretical models describe this evolution using the framework of thin accretion disks, where the vertical thickness of the disk is small compared with its radius. In fact:

“Protoplanetary disks are observed to be geometrically thin, in the sense that the vertical scale height (h r), and they typically have inferred masses (M_{disk} M_*). These properties imply that models of disk evolution should be grounded within the theory of thin accretion disks.” (Armitage, 2011)

Angular momentum transport may occur through several mechanisms, including turbulence, magnetic processes, and disk winds.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Gravitational instability
In sufficiently massive and cold disks, self-gravity can become important. In this regime, gravitational instabilities can generate spiral density patterns that redistribute mass and angular momentum within the disk.
These spiral structures may also concentrate solid particles and potentially contribute to the formation of planetesimals.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Rings
These systems display a central cavity surrounded by a bright ring of dust emission. This morphology is typical of transition disks.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Gaps
This is the most common substructure pattern observed in millimeter continuum images. It consists of concentric bright rings separated by darker gaps.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Arcs
Some disks show asymmetric arc-shaped structures, which may correspond to vortices or local dust concentrations.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Spirals
Large-scale spiral patterns can arise from gravitational instability or perturbations produced by massive companions or forming planets.
